title: New York Fed's Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit Shows Continued Signs of Healing in Consumer Credit Markets

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed released its Q2 2011 Household Debt and Credit Report showing continued healing in consumer credit markets, with modest increases in consumer and bank willingness to borrow and lend. Total consumer debt remained essentially flat, down $50 billion, suggesting the pace of deleveraging that began in late 2008 has slowed. Delinquency rates continued to improve, with both delinquent and seriously delinquent balances remaining 15 percent below year-ago levels.
key_points: Total consumer debt essentially flat, down just $50 billion—effectively a repeat of the previous quarter, suggesting the pace of consumer deleveraging has slowed
Non-real estate indebtedness fell $10 billion (0.4%) to $2.28 trillion, now 9.5% below its Q4 2008 peak
Credit card limits increased for the second consecutive quarter by $60 billion (approximately 2%)
Open credit card accounts jumped by 10 million to 389 million
New foreclosure notations fell 22.8% from Q1 2011; new bankruptcies fell 23.8% from Q2 2010
Both delinquent and seriously delinquent balances remain 15 percent below year-ago levels
why_it_matters: This report provides quarterly insight into U.S. household credit health—a key variable for consumer spending outlook and broader economic recovery. The slowing of deleveraging suggests the consumer deleveraging cycle may be stabilizing, which has implications for economic growth trajectory and credit market conditions. Note: This is a press release summary of the full report, not the detailed data set.
macro_context: The report indicates household sector deleveraging has slowed significantly from its peak pace in late 2008, suggesting consumer credit markets are healing. This has implications for consumer spending capacity and broader economic recovery. Andrew Haughwout noted it remains unclear whether this is a permanent or temporary break in the decline of total consumer debt. Source age (2011) limits relevance to current market conditions.
market_structure_context: The topic 'market structure' is misaligned with this source. This content covers household debt and credit conditions (macroeconomic consumer credit data) rather than market microstructure, trading venue analysis, market making, or financial market infrastructure.

issues_found: Source is from August 2011—approximately 14.7 years old—significantly beyond the 90-day threshold with limited archival value for current market analysis
